children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 154 – Financing of the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo – Report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

Recommendation of the Advisory Committee for 2026/27

children.text: 1.	During its consideration of the report on the financing of the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo (UNMIK) (A/80/615), the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions met in person and online with representatives of the Secretary-General, who provided additional information and clarification, concluding with written responses received on 31 March 2026. The comments and recommendations of the Committee on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations are contained in its report (A/80/604), and those pertaining to the findings and recommendations of the Board of Auditors on United Nations peacekeeping operations for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 can be found in its related report (A/80/633).

text: II.	Budget performance report for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025

children.text: 2.	By its resolution 78/302, the General Assembly appropriated an amount of $43,684,700 gross ($39,378,900 net) for the maintenance of the Mission for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025. Expenditure for the period totalled $43,659,600 gross ($38,927,500 net), for a budget implementation rate of 99.9 per cent, which reflected increased requirements for civilian personnel, offset by reduced requirements for operational costs owing to the reprioritization of resources to meet the increased requirements for civilian personnel.

3.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that redeployments in the amount of $1,409,500 to group II (civilian personnel) and group I (military and police personnel) from group III (operational costs) resulted from expenditures in excess of the appropriation primarily due to higher requirements for international staff. The Advisory Committee trusts that more detailed information on redeployments will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report, in particular on activities curtailed and other lower-than-budgeted expenditures that allowed for resources to be redeployed and on criteria used by missions to reprioritize resources.

4.	The Advisory Committee notes from information provided to it that the unliquidated obligations amounted to $470,800 as at 30 June 2025, compared with the amount of $364,800 as at 30 June 2024. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that, as at 31 January 2026, unliquidated obligations had decreased to $26,800 for the 2024/25 period, comprising civilian personnel ($13,900) and operational costs ($12,900).

5.	The unencumbered balance of $25,100 in gross terms represents 0.1 per cent of the overall level of resources approved for the financial period. A detailed analysis of variances is provided in section III of the report of the Secretary-General. Information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that other revenue and adjustments for the 2024/25 period amounted to $81,000, comprising: (a) investment revenue ($100); (b) other/miscellaneous revenue ($18,500), including other revenue ($13,700) and the refund of prior-period expenses ($4,800); and (c) the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($62,400). The Committee notes that the report of the Secretary-General indicates, in paragraphs 67 and 97, that credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates (see para. 39 below).
Matters pertaining to the report of the Board of Auditors

6.	In considering the reports of the Secretary-General on the financing of UNMIK, the Advisory Committee also had before it the report of the Board of Auditors on the accounts of the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the financial period ended 30 June 2025 (A/80/5 (Vol. II)). Regarding UNMIK, the Board reviewed, inter alia, the ongoing cash shortage issues and liquidity management aspects (A/80/5 (Vol. II), chap. II, paras. 177–199, and chap. IV, para. 12). The Advisory Committee trusts that the recommendations of the Board of Auditors will be implemented in a timely manner.

text: III.	Information on performance for the current period

children.text: 7.	With respect to current and projected expenditure for the period from 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, total expenditure amounted to $31,190,400. At the end of the current period, total expenditure was estimated at $39,082,100 with a projected unencumbered balance of $6,896,800, or 15.0 per cent.

8.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, in the 2025/26 contingency plan of UNMIK, a 15 per cent overall budget reduction was implemented through an 18 per cent cut in the number of military and police personnel, a 13 per cent cut in civilian staff levels and a 23 per cent cut in operational costs. Military and police reductions were managed by delaying new deployments after current assignments ended. Civilian reductions were coordinated with an ongoing staffing review. Operational savings came mainly from deferring projects, maintenance, travel and acquisitions – measures deemed reversible when conditions allow. While steps were taken to limit negative effects on mandate delivery, significant impacts included a 25 per cent cut in programmatic and quick-impact projects and a 29 per cent reduction in civilian staff numbers through various separation processes. The Advisory Committee notes that, while UNMIK has been able to preserve operational continuity and maintain core mandate implementation, liquidity challenges constrained its operational capacity. The Committee trusts that all efforts will be undertaken to ensure the security and safety of personnel. The Committee also trusts that an update on and a more detailed assessment of the impact of the implementation of the contingency plan on the Mission’s mandate will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the report and in the next performance report.

9.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 9 February 2026, a total of $3,746,398,000 had been assessed on Member States in respect of UNMIK since its inception. Payments received as at the same date amounted to $3,697,633,000, resulting in an outstanding balance of $48,765,000. The Committee was also informed that, as at 9 February 2026, the cash available to the Mission amounted to $7,861,000, which is insufficient to cover the three-month operating reserve of $11,495,000. The cash balance reflects the borrowing of $31.5 million from the Peacekeeping Reserve Fund. The Advisory Committee notes that outstanding contributions of $48.8 million exceed the 2025/26 appropriation level. The Committee reiterates its concern regarding the serious and persistent challenge of the cash position of the Mission and the continued need for the borrowing of funds by UNMIK to meet its operational requirements. The Committee recalls that the General Assembly has repeatedly urged all Member States to fulfil their financial obligations as set out in the Charter of the United Nations on time, in full and without conditions (see A/79/724, para. 16; see also Assembly resolution 79/305).

10.	With respect to the death and disability compensation, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 12 February 2026, an amount of $1,057,000 had been paid to settle 30 claims since the inception of the Mission, with no claims pending.

text: IV.	Proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

children.text: A.	Mandate and planning assumptions

11.	The mandate of UNMIK was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1244 (1999). The planning assumptions and mission support initiatives of the Mission for the 2026/27 period are summarized in section I.C of the report of the Secretary-General on the proposed budget for the Mission (A/80/615).
Impact of the UN80 Initiative and culture of efficiency

12.	Information on efficiency gains is provided in section II.C of the report of the Secretary-General. The report attributes the 2026/27 estimated efficiency gains to the implementation of the civilian staffing review recommendations. Estimated efficiencies for 2026/27 represent a 11.9 per cent reduction compared to the approved budget for 2025/26, in the context of an overall 8.6 per cent proposed reduction (see para. 14 below). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that UNMIK confirms that the proposed budget, including the efficiency gains, will support the delivery of the UNMIK mandate while also aligning with the UN80 Initiative. The Advisory Committee notes that, while some proposed reductions reflect structural efficiency gains resulting from process improvements, others are driven primarily by liquidity constraints and temporary expenditure compression. The Committee trusts that further information on the connection between the proposed reductions and changes in the expected accomplishments and outputs proposed for the Mission for 2026/27 will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, to allow for a better assessment of the sustainability and long-term implications of such measures on the implementation of the Mission’s mandate. The Committee will further discuss efficiency measures in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
B.	Resource requirements

13.	Total resource requirements in the amount of $42,022,400 requested for the 2026/27 period represent a decrease of $3,956,500, or 8.6 per cent, compared with the provision of $45,978,900 approved for the 2025/26 period (see table below).
Financial resources – (Thousands of United States dollars)
Note: Detailed information on the financial resources proposed and an analysis of variances are provided in sections II and III of the proposed budget (A/80/615).

14.	The decrease is attributable primarily to lower requirements under civilian personnel owing to the proposed net reduction of 21 international and 28 national staff. Information on variances between the apportionment for 2025/26 and the proposed resources for 2026/27 is presented in section III of the proposed budget.
1.	Military and police personnel
a	Represents the highest level of authorized strength.
Delayed deployment factors – (Percentage of approved strength)

15.	The proposed resources for military and police personnel in the amount of $565,800 reflect a reduction of $299,100, or 34.6 per cent, compared with the apportionment for 2025/26. The proposed decrease is mainly attributable to the application of higher vacancy rates for military observers and United Nations police, combined with the lower revised mission subsistence allowance rate effective 1 January 2026 (A/80/615, paras. 75 and 77). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that no resolution has been adopted by the Security Council on the situation in Kosovo since resolution 1244 (1999), which does not contain figures related to uniformed (or any other) personnel. The General Assembly, through the budget process, has approved the numbers of personnel, including the numbers of uniformed personnel. The current strength of the uniformed personnel in UNMIK is in accordance with Assembly resolution 77/311, by which the Assembly approved the deployment of 10 United Nations police officers and 8 military observers, as proposed by the Secretary-General in his 2023/24 budget report for UNMIK (A/77/723). The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on planned deployment and the proposed delayed deployment factor will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
16.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for military and police personnel.
2.	Civilian personnel

17.	The proposed resources for civilian personnel amounting to $32,626,300 reflect a decrease of $4,055,700, or 11.1 per cent, compared with the apportionment for 2025/26, mainly due to lower requirements under the international and National Professional Officer staff categories on account of the proposed civilian staffing review recommendations (A/80/615, paras. 79 and 81).
Civilian staffing review

18.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the 2025 civilian staffing review – the first for UNMIK since 2015 – evaluated the Mission’s staffing structure in light of evolving peacekeeping needs, financial constraints and the UN80 Initiative. The review was conducted amid significant financial constraints caused by a mandated 15 per cent expenditure reduction across peacekeeping missions. It served to examine restructuring options, including transferring non-location-dependent functions to the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda, to enhance efficiency. UNMIK embraced the civilian staffing review’s recommendations to modernize and restructure the Mission. The Advisory Committee notes the efforts of UNMIK in identifying efficiencies in its staffing review, in accordance with the spirit and goals of the UN80 Initiative.
Recommendations on posts and positions

19.	A total of 310 civilian posts and positions are proposed for 2026/27, comprising 90 international staff, 194 national staff (33 National Professional Officers and 161 national General Service staff) and 26 United Nations Volunteers, reflecting a net decrease of 46 posts compared with the budget approved for 2024/25. As a result of the recommendations arising from the staffing review, the 2026/27 budget includes the following changes: (a) abolishment of 49 posts comprising 21 international posts (2 D-1, 2 P-5, 4 P-4 and 13 Field Service level), 2 National Professional Officer posts and 26 national General Service posts; (b) establishment of 1 National Professional Officer post and 2 international United Nations Volunteer positions; (c) conversion of 2 Field Service posts to 1 National Professional Officer and 1 national General Service post; (d) redeployment of 31 posts, including 5 international posts, 3 national Professional Officers, 22 national General Service posts and 1 United Nations Volunteer position; and (e) reassignment of 24 posts, including 7 international posts, 1 National Professional Officer post, 13 national General Service posts and 3 United Nations Volunteer positions (ibid., para 30).

20.	The Advisory Committee notes, under executive direction and management in the second table of the report of the Secretary-General, that the title of the Office of Community Support and Facilitation is to be changed to the Office of Community Support. The Advisory Committee trusts that further clarity and justification regarding the proposed change will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report.

21.	The report of the Secretary-General indicates that the civilian staffing review recommended movement of the Mission Support Division to a two-pillar structure (ibid., para. 47). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the civilian staffing review recommended that UNMIK merge the two pillars but move the Procurement Section to the Operations and Resource Management pillar, thereby maintaining the necessary internal controls, while keeping all current supply chain and service delivery functions under UNMIK. The Advisory Committee stresses the importance of preserving the segregation of duties, and of adequate risk monitoring to ensure that the streamlined structure does not undermine internal controls and operational capacities. The Committee looks forward to an assessment of the performance of and challenges experienced by the missions with regard to the implementation of their two-pillar support structures in the next report on the overview of peacekeeping operations.

22.	The report of the Secretary-General proposes the conversion of two international Field Service posts into national positions: an Administrative Assistant in Mitrovica and a Human Resources Assistant in Pristina (ibid., paras. 45 and 50). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the percentage of national staff approved for 2025/26 was 66 per cent and that, under the proposed budget for 2026/27, the percentage of national staff would increase to 68 per cent.
Vacant posts

23.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Mission had 120 vacant posts as at 28 February 2026 (4 D-1, 5 P-5, 8 P-4, 8 P-3, 2 P-2, 14 Field Service, 12 National Professional Officers and 67 national General Service). UNMIK had eight posts (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 Field Service and 5 General Service) vacant for more than two years, of which five (1 P-4, 1 Field Service and 3 General Service) are proposed for abolishment in 2026/27, one (General Service) is proposed for reassignment in 2026/27 and another two (1 P-3 and 1 General Service) are proposed for retention. The Advisory Committee trusts that information on the status of vacant posts will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission and that all long-vacant posts will continue to be critically reviewed by the Mission (see also para. 19 above).

24.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that there were eight posts encumbered by staff with approved requests for the early separation programme as at 31 December 2025. The Advisory Committee trusts that detailed and updated information on all staff of the Mission approved for the early separation programme, as well as on the related costs, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission.

25.	The Advisory Committee recalls that, in paragraph 9 of General Assembly resolution 79/305, the Assembly had decided to reassign the vacant P-3 level post of Rule of Law Officer (Judicial Affairs Officer) in the Justice and Correction Section of the Office of Rule of Law as a P-3 level post of Civil Affairs Officer (Property Rights) in the Office of Community Support and Facilitation. The Committee also recalls paragraph 9 of Assembly resolution 78/302, by which the Assembly decided to reclassify the post of Civil Affairs Officer in the Office of Community Support and Facilitation in Pristina from the P-4 to the P-5 level. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that the P-3 post of Rule of Law Officer (Judicial Affairs Officer) in the Justice and Correction Section, Office of Rule of Law, had been reassigned as a P-3 post of Civil Affairs Officer (Property Rights) in the Office of Community Support and Facilitation by the Assembly effective 1 July 2025. Following the decision of the Assembly, in its resolution 78/302, to reclassify the post of P-4 Civil Affairs Officer to the P-5 level, the job opening was posted in December 2024, and UNMIK undertook further steps in recruitment through the assessment process. However, due to the peacekeeping-wide hiring freeze effective March 2025, the P-3 and P-5 posts have remained vacant. The Advisory Committee recalls the decisions of the General Assembly in resolutions 78/302 and 79/305 and considers that, as a matter of principle, the decisions of the Assembly should be implemented. The Committee trusts that the Mission will continue with recruitment as expeditiously as possible.
Vacancy rates
(Percentage)

26.	The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide updated information on the latest actual and actual average vacancy rates to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, along with clear justification where the proposed rates differ from the actual rates, including as it relates to the impact of proposed abolishments and realistic projected recruitment and potential upcoming vacancies, to the extent possible.
27.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for civilian personnel.
3.	Operational costs – (Thousands of United States dollars)

28.	The proposed resources for operational costs for the 2026/27 period represent a net increase of $398,300, or 4.7 per cent, compared with the resources approved for the 2025/26 period. The proposed net increase for 2026/27 reflects higher requirements for facilities and infrastructure, programmatic activities and quick-impact projects, offset in part by proposed decreases under other object classes.
Facilities and infrastructure

29.	A provision of $2,826,800 is proposed for 2026/27, representing an increase of $312,300, or 12.4 per cent, compared with the approved level of $2,514,500 for 2025/26. Expenditure in 2024/25 amounted to $2,282,200 against the appropriation of $2,870,300 and in 2023/24 amounted to $2,208,600 against the appropriation of $2,685,800. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the proposed increase in the 2026/27 facilities and infrastructure budget of UNMIK is due to the planned relocation of the Mitrovica Regional Office. The move is necessary because the current site has been rezoned for high-density residential construction, making it unsuitable for continued office operations. The additional resources will cover relocation and rental costs to secure a suitable, compliant and cost-effective space that preserves mandate delivery and minimizes operational risk. The Committee was further informed that the Mission is reviewing its physical footprint – including office space, warehousing and facilities – to align with UN80 Initiative principles and boost operational efficiencies based on current usage, future needs and reduced personnel. The review is aimed at achieving a fit-for-purpose, cost-effective set-up that reflects evolving programmatic/logistical demands, with opportunities to consolidate space across duty stations alongside other United Nations entities for better utilization. The Advisory Committee looks forward to the results of the review of the Mission’s physical footprint, including information on any related efficiencies, being provided in the next report.
Petrol, oil and lubricants

30.	A provision of $128,473 is proposed for fuel, oil and lubricants for the 2026/27 period, reflecting an increase of $1,108, or 0.9 per cent, owing mainly to changes in fuel costs. The total amount is budgeted across two budget classes: facilities and infrastructure ($84,100, with an increase of $2,345); and ground transportation ($44,373, with a decrease of $1,237). The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on fuel costs will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report. The Committee further discusses fuel costs and management in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Quick-impact projects

31.	The proposed resources amount to $300,000 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $100,000, or 50.0 per cent, compared with the approved level of $200,000 for 2025/26, while expenditure amounted to $30,000 as at 28 February 2026. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, in the context of the 2025/26 contingency plan, the Mission had to implement a 25 per cent reduction in programmatic activities and quick-impact projects (see para. 8 above). The Committee was further informed that the proposed increase in funding for quick-impact projects from $200,000 in 2025/26 to $300,000 in 2026/27 will enable UNMIK to expand swift, targeted interventions such as small infrastructure works, renovations and community facilities that address urgent local needs. Quick-impact projects, introduced by UNMIK in 2025/26 and of which one is already in progress and two are forthcoming, are designed to complement ongoing programmatic and community trust-building activities by delivering rapid, targeted responses to urgent local priorities. The Committee was also informed that UNMIK oversees quick-impact projects through a structured review and monitoring process led by the quick-impact projects review committee, chaired by the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General and involving all relevant UNMIK sections. The Advisory Committee notes that this is the second year in which quick-impact projects are implemented by the Mission and trusts that UNMIK will continue to ensure that the projects benefit the local population, as intended, including through hard-walled projects that leave a positive legacy of the Mission in the local communities. The Committee further trusts that projects will continue to be implemented in accordance with existing policies and guidelines and that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next proposed budget report
32.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for operational costs.
Environmental initiatives

33.	In his report, the Secretary-General indicates that the installation of the solar-powered system at the Mission headquarters in Pristina was deferred due to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for civilian personnel. The Mission remains committed to minimizing its environmental footprint by maintaining solar-powered systems at its Pristina headquarters and regional repeater sites while implementing initiatives from the environmental action plan, the 2025–2027 water conservation programme and the waste management plan (A/80/615, para. 16). The Advisory Committee trusts that the Mission will improve its environmental footprint and that further efficiency gains generated from the photovoltaic power plants, as well as other environmental initiatives based on cost-benefit analysis, will be reflected in future reports. The Committee further discusses environmental activities and energy efficiency in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Management of sea containers

34.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that UNMIK currently operates 42 containers, all in active and serviceable condition – 22 used for regular storage, 5 for information technology and communications equipment, 3 for armoury storage, 9 for accommodation and 3 for ablution. The Committee further discusses the management of sea containers in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Equitable geographical representation and gender parity

35.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 December 2025, UNMIK comprised 228 staff at all levels. Among the 88 UNMIK international staff, 50 nationalities were represented. Of the total 228 staff, 91 were female and 137 were male.
36.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was provided with the nationalities of senior-level staff as at 31 December 2025:

table_content.text: France, Australia, Portugal, United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, Austria

children.text: 37.	International UNMIK staff were from the following regional groups: African States (12), Asia-Pacific States (15), Eastern European States (20) and Western European and other States (34), as well as the United States of America (7).

38.	The Advisory Committee trusts that UNMIK will further pursue its efforts to promote equitable geographical representation and gender parity. The Committee further discusses equitable geographical representation and gender parity in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

39.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of UNMIK for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 are indicated in section IV of the budget performance report (A/80/615). The Advisory Committee notes the unencumbered balance of $25,100 for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025, as well as other revenue amounting to $81,000 from investment revenue ($100), other/miscellaneous revenue ($18,500) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($62,400) for the period ended 30 June 2025. The Committee also notes the proposal of the Secretary-General to place the credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates. The Committee considers that this is a policy matter to be decided by the General Assembly. The Advisory Committee makes further comments in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

40.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of the Mission for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 are indicated in section IV of the proposed budget (A/80/615). Taking into account its observations and recommendations above, the Advisory Committee recommends that the General Assembly appropriate the amount of $42,022,400 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027.
